summary: Apple has stopped bundling Flash Player with Mac OS X, starting with the new MacBook Air models and expanding across the entire Mac product line. Unlike iOS where Flash is completely blocked, Mac users can still download and install Flash themselves from Adobe. This ends a bundling arrangement dating back to approximately Mac OS 8.6 and shifts responsibility for Flash security updates entirely to Adobe. Gruber notes this could gradually erode Flash penetration on the Mac, especially as users grow accustomed to the Mac App Store as their sole software installation method. The move represents another step in Apple's systematic distancing from Flash, though framed diplomatically as ensuring users get the most current version directly from Adobe.
key_points: Apple confirmed to Gruber that all new Macs will ship without Flash Player in the coming weeks, not just the MacBook Air
The unbundling absolves Apple of responsibility for distributing Flash security updates, following the Snow Leopard controversy where Macs shipped with a vulnerable Flash version
Flash penetration on the Mac could decline over time, especially as users adopt the Mac App Store which excludes plugins
Unlike iOS's outright ban, this is positioned as a user-choice situation — download it yourself if you want it
Apple's battery life tests for the new Air were performed with Flash installed, so the gains aren't attributable to Flash's absence
money_quotes: One, however, was not: a system that ships without Flash Player.
Existing Macs currently in the retail channel naturally still have Flash Player preinstalled, but in the coming weeks, all new Macs will begin shipping without Flash Player.
This also absolves Apple of responsibility for the distribution of Flash Player security updates.
Henceforth, Flash Player security updates for Mac OS X are Adobe's problem, not Apple's.
There are a lot of Mac users who've never known the joy of using an Adobe software installer, but who soon will.

Maybe everyone (or just about everyone) will go ahead and download and install it themselves. But maybe not — especially if, in the coming future, Mac users become accustomed to installing third-party software solely through the Mac App Store.
key_insight: Apple's decision to stop bundling Flash with the Mac — while still allowing its installation — is a strategically elegant middle ground that distances Apple from Flash's security liabilities while letting market forces and the Mac App Store gradually diminish Flash's relevance.
